card.title: The Birth of the Little Black Dress
card.teaser: Black used to mean mourning. Then one page in Vogue, 1926, declared a simple black shift 'a sort of uniform' for all women.
card.coverImagePrompt: A 1920s Parisian atelier mannequin wearing a sleek black crepe chemise dress, soft art-deco wall lamps, pearls draped over a chair, glossy editorial lighting

lessons.title: One sketch that changed everything
lessons.body: In October 1926, American Vogue published a small line drawing of a simple black dress with long sleeves and a dropped waist. The caption called it 'The Chanel 'Ford' — the frock that all the world will wear.' Vogue was comparing it to the Model T: a democratic, mass-produced object of perfect function. That one sketch launched a garment that is still in nearly every wardrobe a hundred years later.

visual.imagePrompt: An aged page from 1926 Vogue magazine showing a delicate ink sketch of a black dress, captioned in period typography, sepia archival feel

lessons.title: Why black was radical
lessons.body: Before the 1920s, black clothing in Western fashion meant one thing: you were in mourning. Widows wore it for up to two years; even servants wore it as a signal of rank. For a designer to propose black as glamorous daywear was a cultural provocation — like turning a hospital gown into eveningwear. Coco Chanel reportedly said, 'I imposed black; it's still going strong today.'

keyTerms.definition: The strict black wardrobe Victorian and Edwardian women wore after a family death
A loose, straight 1920s cut that skimmed rather than cinched the body

visual.imagePrompt: A split illustration: a Victorian widow in heavy black mourning crepe beside a 1920s flapper in a chic short black dress, contrasting the same color, same century

lessons.title: Recession-proof genius
lessons.body: Chanel's design was almost shockingly simple — no corset, no excess fabric, just a clean shape anyone could copy. That was the point. When the Great Depression hit in 1929, women could buy one quality black dress, swap the accessories, and suddenly have a wardrobe. It was fashion designed for a world that was running out of money but not out of occasions.

visual.imagePrompt: A single black dress on a wooden hanger beside a row of belts, scarves, and pearl strands, minimalist boutique aesthetic

lessons.title: From Chanel to Givenchy to Audrey
lessons.body: The little black dress was cemented as icon in 1961, when Hubert de Givenchy dressed Audrey Hepburn in a floor-length black Italian satin sheath for the opening of 'Breakfast at Tiffany's'. That single costume became the most photographed dress of the 20th century — the original sold at auction in 2006 for £467,200 to fund an Indian children's charity.
lessons.funFact: Givenchy actually designed three versions of the dress; the Hollywood costume department replaced the original with a more modest copy because the slit was considered too high.

visual.imagePrompt: An elegant woman with an updo and long gloves silhouetted outside a glittering Fifth Avenue jewelry window at dawn, cinematic mid-century colors

lessons.title: Why it never dies
lessons.body: The little black dress survives because it does something most fashion refuses to do: get out of the way. It hides dirt, flatters most figures, photographs well, and can be made to look cheap or priceless depending on context. In a century that has demolished almost every other dress code, the LBD is the rare garment still accepted almost everywhere — funerals included.

quiz.question: What did Vogue nickname Chanel's 1926 black dress?
quiz.explanation: Vogue compared its universal appeal to Ford's Model T, calling it 'the frock that all the world will wear.'

quiz.question: Before the 1920s, what did a black dress usually signal in Western Europe?
quiz.explanation: Black was the strict color code of Victorian and Edwardian mourning, which made Chanel's daywear use of it scandalous.

quiz.question: Who designed the iconic LBD worn by Audrey Hepburn in 'Breakfast at Tiffany's'?
quiz.explanation: Givenchy designed Hepburn's satin sheath; the original sold at Christie's in 2006 for over £460,000.

sources.title: The Gospel According to Coco Chanel
